fields.Name: Binary classification (yes/no)
fields.Description: Predict one of two outcomes (e.g., will renew / won't renew, will lapse / won't lapse, will attend / won't attend).
fields.Guidance: The most common Predictive Studio shape. Churn, renewal, lapse risk, lead scoring, and attendee-return are all this same binary-classification shape — the business label differs but the algorithm suitability does not, which is why guidance keys off this scenario rather than the business name. Favor algorithms that output a well-calibrated probability and rank-order well (AUC).

fields.Name: Regression (predict a number)
fields.Description: Predict a continuous numeric value (e.g., expected lifetime value, days until next purchase, projected gift amount).
fields.Guidance: Use when the target is a number rather than a yes/no. Tree ensembles handle non-linearities and interactions automatically; linear/ridge is the interpretable baseline. Logistic regression does not apply here.

fields.Name: Interpretability required
fields.Description: A stakeholder must be able to see exactly why each prediction was made (per-feature coefficients / a defensible explanation).
fields.Guidance: When a human needs to defend or audit individual predictions (regulatory, fairness, exec sign-off), prefer models with simple, signed coefficients (Logistic for classification, Ridge for regression). Tree ensembles give feature importances but not simple per-prediction reasons; deep nets are effectively opaque.

fields.Name: Minimal tuning (business-user)
fields.Description: A non-expert user wants a strong result with sensible defaults and little or no hyperparameter tuning.
fields.Guidance: Favor algorithms that are robust out-of-the-box and forgiving of defaults. Random Forest and linear models rarely blow up; gradient boosting is strong but more sensitive to learning-rate/depth; MLPs need the most care. This scenario weights 'works well untuned' over peak ceiling.

fields.Name: Large/wide dataset (speed)
fields.Description: Many rows and/or many features, where training and scoring speed and memory efficiency matter.
fields.Guidance: Favor algorithms that scale to large/wide data efficiently. LightGBM is built for this (histogram-based, fast, low memory); XGBoost and linear models also scale well; Random Forest can get memory-heavy; MLP training cost grows quickly.

fields.Name: Embedding/LLM-feature-heavy
fields.Description: Features are dominated by dense high-dimensional vectors (text/entity embeddings, LLM-derived numeric features).
fields.Guidance: When most features are dense embedding dimensions, models that exploit smooth high-dimensional geometry shine. MLPs are well-suited to dense vectors; gradient boosting still does implicit feature selection across many dimensions and remains a strong, safer default. Pure linear models underfit rich embedding interactions.

fields.Name: Small dataset
fields.Description: Relatively few training rows, where high-capacity models would overfit.
fields.Guidance: With limited data, prefer lower-variance models that resist overfitting: regularized linear/logistic and Random Forest generalize well on small samples. Gradient boosting can work with careful early stopping; MLPs typically overfit small data badly.
